title: How to minimise facial sagging for patients! (It's not JUST about injection technique)
filename: How to minimise facial sagging for patients! (It_s not JUST about injection technique).en.vtt
excerpt: How to treat midface sagging. In this video, I'm going to teach you exactly how I treat the midface. In fact, let's dive straight into it. I'll show you where I injected this patient to get this result.

segments.text: How to treat midface sagging. In this video, I'm going to teach you exactly how I treat the midface. In fact, let's dive straight into it. I'll show you where I injected this patient to get this result. I injected on top of her zygoma, anterior to her zygoma, lateral to her zygoma, all on the perryioium, and I did a second pass with a canula to make it look just right. In this video, I'm going to teach you how to get the diagnosis correct, how to communicate with your patients, how to create a treatment plan, how to use the anatomy to get it right, and how to actually deliver the procedure to get the best possible result. A typical patient will attend saying something like, "I look either saggy or tired or miserable." It's the clinician's job to link this back to the anatomy and create a treatment plan that will solve those problems. By the end of this video, you're going to know exactly why I treated this patient in the way that I did. You're going to understand how to speak to your patients differently and interestingly how to diagnose your patients when they are sending you the wrong signal. Patients do this weird thing in consultations that when you understand it will help you get the diagnosis right and give them the

treatment they really want. So what do we need to know before you start injecting? We have to get the diagnosis right. I teach that there are actually three diagnoses that need to be made. The first is the patient's story. What is it they're actually hoping to get out of this treatment? Once you know what they want, it's easier to guide them to get the right answer. The second thing is the anatomy. What are the structures of the face that change as you age and therefore which ones do we need to treat to get the result the patient wants? And finally, there's the aesthetics. You can't interpret the anatomy and change the anatomy without understanding what good aesthetics are. So often this is where the patient starts. They want to look better and they need an expert to help them guide them to get that result. Now, before you can start teaching your patients about what the ideal cheek looks like, we need to understand the types of presentations that they come with that actually mean I need a cheek treatment. Now, this starts with understanding that patients communicate, like all people, 70% non-verbally. You will notice a huge number of your patients describing what they want, even if they're talking about a different

area of the face while doing this. This is one of the telltale signs that they need lateral and medial cheek volume to correct a problem because subconsciously they've connected this tissue here with what's happening in their lower face and the lower cheek. So, this is often one of the first things that I can see during a consultation that tells me to zone in on the midface. There are also really common important words that patients use such as saggginess or looking miserable. If they use these terms, they're often talking about what is happening in the midface. And this is where your brain should immediately go to start thinking about how am I going to link it in their minds to a cheek treatment in a way that leaves them absolutely confident it's the right way to go. But there's one step before you go down the route of treatment planning, which is we need to rule out certain types of treatment that you wouldn't want to do in the cheek. The most important of these is previously overtreated cheek treatments. There are unfortunately many patients who've had cheek treatments again and again for many years without a more holistic assessment. And those patients might need other areas treating or no

treatment at all rather than treating the cheeks themselves. We need to quite quickly rule out treatment in patients who don't need it because it changes the conversation quite early on and it helps you get a better result for them long term. There may also be existing proportion issues. For example, patients who already have quite wide cheeks whether they have had treatment or not may not be suitable for additional volume without it changing the proportions of their midface in a way that isn't actually an upgrade. So understanding proportions is also key. Now that you are clear that the patient needs treatment and there are no potential aesthetic risks from doing the treatment. There's always a psychological assessment we include too. Are they likely to respond positively and be happy after the treatment is a simple way of saying is this person coming across as well oriented, happy, healthy psychologically and simply improving themselves rather than someone who may be dysmorphic. Let's take a look at our case study. In this case, like most patients, the broader goal is simply to look great for your age. look fresher, maybe less tired, happier, those sorts of terms that a good clinician then relates to a treatment

plan. So, as soon as I see this patient's face, I can see that there's a potential to improve her midface, and this may have a knock-on effect on improving her lower face. It certainly makes it a lot easier to treat the lower face when the midface is well supported. I'm looking now at thinking about the fat pads that lie underneath the skin. Remember, we're trying to pull three different diagnoses together. She may feel tired. That's the psychology. Have a fat pad that is shrunk just lateral to her eye. That's the anatomy. And then this causes a shadow which is associated with tiredness or saggginess. That's the aesthetic. With all three of these in alignment, the treatment plan becomes much easier. This patient needs a great deal of support in the midface. If you look at the overall shape and proportion of her face, the projecting points of her cheek, the lines that define the difference between cheek and jawline are relatively soft and hard to see. and there are disruptions to the planes that connect her lower eyelid to her cheek. This causes a visual clutter. If I can improve the structure and integrate the shape better into the rest of the face,

she's going to look happier, brighter, healthier, and more lifted. And this all comes from improving the cheek. So, with a clear aesthetic goal in mind, the injection strategy then starts to come next. How do we create this result? How much volume do we need? And how do we deliver it safely? and effectively. My preferred way of injecting cheeks is to be deep on the perryioium. I want filler to stay in position for many years and not to lose its shape or to become puffy and cause edema. In my experience, this is best done by injecting deeper on the perryostium, hiding the product under many layers of fat on a very stable surface. But before doing the injection itself, I need to be aware of the three-dimensional shape I'm creating in the cheek. Cheeks are often undertreated anteriorly. It's very common because female cheeks are laterally dominant to see injections on the lateral aspect of the zygoma. But many of your patients you'll find are lacking anterior projection. They essentially have shadows or a flatness to their face that we can correct with an injection

straight onto perryioium that pushes the cheek forward. As soon as you've done this, you may see an increase in disruption in the planes of the face. So, as soon as you have a defining point that's better defined, you may see emptiness either lateral to it on the angle of the zygoma or in the shadow that runs underneath the eye. As I watch myself injecting in this video, I see the missing pieces start to emerge. So, as you project the anterior cheek forward, you see a loss of the projecting plane underneath the eye. It also highlights to my attention that the angle of the cheek isn't quite wide enough. So, this becomes an injection that we do at the angle of the cheek. And then of course the most important aspect for a feminine cheek is the lateral projection. This is what separates male and female cheeks from each other. It's the dominance of the lateral projection here. Once you've done those defining points and lines, it then becomes very clear that the inferior plane underneath the cheek can look a little bit empty. It almost looks like someone's had too much of the buckle fat removed and they end up with a defining line that looks essentially gaunt. And this is where a canula

treatment comes into its own. We certainly don't want to be injecting deep with a needle in this area because your transverse facial artery and the important nerves of your face will run underneath the zygoma. In this area, I switch to a canula at middle depth and I feather this in gently to create a nice smooth transition between the projecting points of the cheek and the inverse defining line of the mid cheek. All the way through while doing a treatment, I'm continuously assessing and making small adjustments. There are several principles of beauty that I use day in and day out. We want to see clearly the structure underneath of a healthy cheek, but we also want that structure to be blended in seamlessly with the rest of the face. We don't want harsh transitions or over definition. This is one of the signs sometimes of overt treatment and one of the subtle ways that a poor treatment can actually make young patients look older because you're not replacing volume in a holistic way. All of the core principles in this video will apply to an entire facial treatment. And if you're interested in really mastering full face treatments, don't forget to sign up to my up andcoming webinar where I have got a

very exciting new framework that I believe is the missing piece in medical aesthetics training. It also gives you an opportunity to join my Profinity membership. In fact, in this video, you'll see one of our members who came to shadow me while we did this treatment together. It's one of the many opportunities that come through being a member of Profinity.
